title: End of module: What Is AI?

questions.text: Which of the following best defines artificial intelligence, as used in this course?
choices: A system that is conscious and self-aware
A computational system that performs tasks which would require human intelligence if a person did them
Any robot with a physical body
A program guaranteed never to make a mistake
questions.explanation: AI is defined by *what a system does* — performing tasks (perception, language, reasoning, decision-making) that would need human intelligence if a person did them — not by consciousness, embodiment, or infallibility.

questions.text: A spam filter that blocks any email containing a fixed, hand-written list of banned words (e.g. "lottery winner") is an example of…
choices: rule-based (symbolic) AI
learning-based AI
general AI
not AI at all, since it is too simple
questions.explanation: A human decided the exact trigger words in advance and the filter simply follows that fixed list — the hallmark of rule-based (symbolic) AI. It still counts as AI: it automates a task (deciding spam vs. not) that otherwise needs human judgement.

questions.text: An image-recognition system trained on millions of labelled photographs to distinguish cats from dogs is an example of…
choices: rule-based (symbolic) AI
learning-based AI
general AI
the Turing test
questions.explanation: Nobody hand-writes rules for recognising a cat in a photo; the system's parameters are adjusted automatically from many labelled training examples, which is the defining feature of learning-based AI.

questions.text: Which of the following are true characteristics of narrow (weak) AI?
choices: It is designed and trained to perform one specific task well
It possesses flexible, general reasoning that transfers across any domain
It describes essentially all AI systems currently in everyday use
It has already been achieved in the form of a fully general household robot
questions.explanation: Narrow AI does one job and cannot generalise beyond it (option 1), and it is what virtually all deployed AI is today (option 3). Flexible cross-domain reasoning (option 2) and a fully general household robot (option 4) both describe general AI, which does not exist.

questions.text: What is the name for the hypothetical kind of AI that would have flexible, human-like intelligence able to learn or perform virtually any intellectual task?
questions.explanation: This is **general AI** (also called strong AI, or artificial general intelligence, AGI) — an aspirational goal, not a system that exists today.

questions.text: Rule-based (symbolic) AI systems typically work by…
choices: automatically adjusting their own parameters as they see more data
following explicit rules or logic that a human programmer wrote in advance
requiring no programming of any kind
training a neural network on labelled examples
questions.explanation: In rule-based AI, a human expert encodes explicit rules or logical conditions (e.g. if/then statements), and the program follows exactly those rules — nothing is learned from data.

questions.text: Compared with learning-based AI, a key weakness of rule-based AI is that…
choices: it cannot run on modern computer hardware
it tends to be brittle: it struggles with situations its author did not explicitly anticipate
it always needs a very large training dataset to function at all
its decisions can never be explained or inspected by a human
questions.explanation: Because every case must be anticipated and encoded by hand, rule-based systems are brittle outside the situations their rules were written for. (Needing a large dataset and being hard to inspect are, if anything, weaknesses of learning-based systems, not rule-based ones.)

questions.text: Which of the following are genuine weaknesses of learning-based AI, compared with hand-written rules?
choices: It can behave as a "black box" whose decision-making is hard for a human to inspect or explain
It can reproduce and amplify biases present in its training data
It always outputs a fully explainable, human-readable set of rules
It needs a large, representative set of training examples to perform well
questions.explanation: Learning-based systems are often opaque (option 1), can inherit biased training data (option 2), and need substantial representative data (option 4). Option 3 is false — the opposite is true: learning-based systems typically do *not* produce a transparent, human-readable rule set, which is exactly why they can be a black box.

questions.text: In Alan Turing's imitation game (the Turing test), a machine is judged to have "passed" if…
choices: it can multiply large numbers faster than a human
a human judge, conversing by text with both a human and the machine, cannot reliably tell which is which
it has a humanoid physical appearance
it can defeat any human opponent at chess
questions.explanation: The test is purely behavioural and conversational: if the judge cannot reliably distinguish the machine's text responses from the human's, the machine passes — nothing is asked about arithmetic speed, appearance, or game-playing skill.

questions.text: Which of the following is a genuine limitation of the Turing test as a measure of intelligence?
choices: It is physically impossible for any computer to attempt it
Passing it shows only that a system can convincingly imitate conversation, not that it genuinely understands or reasons
It can only ever be judged by professional computer scientists
It requires the machine being tested to have a camera
questions.explanation: The test measures indistinguishable conversational behaviour, not genuine understanding, reasoning, or consciousness — a purely rule-based system with no real understanding (like ELIZA) can fool some judges, which is exactly why the test is an imperfect proxy for intelligence.

questions.text: In what year did Alan Turing publish "Computing Machinery and Intelligence", the paper that proposed what became known as the Turing test?
questions.explanation: Turing's paper was published in 1950 in the journal *Mind*.

questions.text: In what year was the Dartmouth workshop held, at which the term "artificial intelligence" was coined?
questions.explanation: The Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence, organised by John McCarthy and colleagues, was held in the summer of 1956 and is credited with coining the field's name.

questions.text: Deep Blue, the chess computer that beat world champion Garry Kasparov in 1997, relied mainly on…
choices: a hand-crafted position-evaluation function combined with brute-force search — a rule-based (symbolic) approach
a deep neural network trained entirely by self-play, with no hand-coded chess knowledge
a large language model fine-tuned on chess commentary
trial-and-error guessing with no evaluation of positions at all
questions.explanation: Deep Blue combined a chess-position evaluation function tuned by programmers and grandmasters with a fast search through possible move sequences — a classic rule-based/symbolic approach, not a learning-based one.

questions.text: Unlike Deep Blue, AlphaZero's evaluation of chess positions was…
choices: hand-written by a team of chess grandmasters
learned automatically through millions of games of self-play, given only the rules of chess
looked up from a fixed table of opening moves and nothing else
identical in design to Deep Blue's evaluation function
questions.explanation: AlphaZero was given only the rules of chess and learned which positions were strong purely through self-play — a learning-based approach, in contrast with Deep Blue's hand-crafted, rule-based evaluation function.
